It also doesn't constantly shove crap like the AI DJ to the front page and other useless features that distract from just getting to the music I want. The only downside is that it doesn't have podcasts, but I really enjoy it so far.

I still buy cds of artists. I have listened to those albums so many times I can recite the track list in chronilogical order. Some albums I can tell you the exact day it came out, down to the day of the week. I only used spotify to stream entire albums when I could not find a physical copy of the album. Even then, they were on repeat. I agree that its super easy to tune out the music more though. I feel like I pay attention more when I am listening to physical media. I will get off my soap box of why I like physical media better.

I listen to full albums at least 80% of the time on Spotify, so I don't think I fall victim to as much of the random crap they try to toss my way. I do think that for most listeners, they encourage random consumption to the point where they are hoping the masses will be fine with lifeless AI music. People knock music fans who obsess over a handful of artists that they love while not giving many others a chance, but that incredible connection between the artists and the fans is really the best aspect of the entire experience. The allegiance that fans have to acts like Taylor Swift, The Grateful Dead, and The Beatles is amazing. It's that connection that the streamers are not so subtly trying to eradicate. They are trying to portray the human creativity element of pop music as being completely trivial. That doesn't seem possible since the art depends on the emotion that went into creating the songs, generating an emotional response from the listener. It really has to be personal on both ends to work.

Respect to XuiXiu for conducting themselves with moral integrity and also respect to Antony for bringing important topics to the forefront of music discussion.  Musicians, music journalists and fans need to work together to move forward towards a more inclusive, democratic and moral industry. Billionaires have proven time and time again that they are not nice people so it’s time for us to stop subscribing (literally) to their bullshit

Physical media still exists, guys. Devices on which to play them are on average pretty cheap (depending on which one you get), you pay a one-off fee & get to keep what you bought, and you don't have to worry about it being taken away from you at any given time because - guess what! - it's a piece of physical media. You get to hold it in your hands. It's yours. IDK how much better off financially it is for the artist, but it's probably better than streaming.

EDIT: I was right about that last part.

4:00 I mean streaming has made music more accessible for me because I had this era where I bought a lot of vinyl but I couldn't sustain it and I couldn't sustain buying so many CDs. I don't have a CD player in my car but I would like to give back to more bands. It's just that my money got tighter cuz my bills went up and it's like really shitty. But I have to remember to buy some merch or some vinyl even if I don't have my setup right now. Like I'm all for supporting artists and buying stuff here and there to directly support artists and I love going to shows but it's just so unsustainable for me to get all of my music through piece mail, CD purchases or buying digital Music Masters. Like I like streaming from a consumer perspective but I also understand how much it's devalued music for artists and it sucks but like at this point I wouldn't be able to do the music exploration that I do if it weren't for streaming services or at least I would just have to settle for what's available for free on YouTube?

"Bands lost the fight against streaming & music has become disposable" This hits in my soul. This is happening with all art, in one way or another. I fear this will happen with books w/ AI as well, and movies/TV have also lost their fight to streaming. Art and books are losing to AI to the point you can't tell if some things are real or not anymore. As someone who has always wanted to be an author, and always looked up to the greats, it is devastating. When is the last time we've seen a book hit in recent years world-wide like the Hunger Games? GoT, Harry Potter, Lord of the Rings all came before, and nothing has reached the same level or topped it since. We are racing toward a future with so much content that offers so little

I do playlists on Spotify (The Sweatdown) and have been since 2018. Barely anyone listens to them but they are still important to me because it's the only thing I've done consistently. I really want to leave Spotify but I need to archive all of my playlists before I do and then find a new platform. What sucks is that my original pick was YouTube and YouTube is getting awful with AI as well. So I don't know where I'll go but I'm planning to leave Spotify by the end of this year.
